news_summary: Artemis II's successful Moon fly-by marks a significant milestone in space exploration, showcasing advanced scientific collaboration and progress.
news_summary_fr: Le survol réussi de la Lune par Artemis II marque une étape importante dans l'exploration spatiale, illustrant la collaboration scientifique de pointe et les progrès réalisés.
news_summary_es: El exitoso sobrevuelo lunar de Artemis II supone un hito importante en la exploración espacial, que pone de manifiesto la colaboración científica avanzada y los avances logrados.

articles.title: I was with Artemis II’s scientists during the Moon fly-by. Here’s what I saw
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 09 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01138-yNature correspondent Alexandra Witze describes the joy and tension at mission control in Houston.
articles.content:
Artemis II science team members Jacob Richardson (left) and Kiarre Dumes react to the astronauts’ observations during the Moon fly-by.Credit: NASA/Luna Posadas Nava

Johnson Space Center, Houston, Texas

On Monday this week, as four humans flew around the far side of the Moon, I entered the heart of the Artemis II mission’s science operations.

Historic Artemis II Moon fly-by: Nature’s live coverage as it happened

The door creaked open, and I slipped into a mesh office chair by the wall. I watched the faces of researchers I knew well: lunar scientists I’d talked to at conferences, met on field trips or followed during the days when Twitter existed. Many were women; many were early in their careers.

Now they were focusing on Marie Henderson, NASA’s deputy lunar-science lead for Artemis II. She stood at the front of the room, beneath a row of display screens that showed a countdown clock and the astronauts inside the spacecraft they had named Integrity, a view of the Moon growing larger in their window. The lunar fly-by — a nearly seven-hour period when the astronauts would observe the Moon as they travelled past it — was about to start.

One by one, Henderson polled the scientists for their ‘go’ or ’no go’ signal. After she’d collected them all, she reached her decision: “We are go for lunar fly-by.” The tension in the room ratcheted up a little bit more.

Lunar rhapsody

In the end, those hours went swimmingly. The Artemis II astronauts — Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch and Jeremy Hansen — pulled off a nearly flawless sequence of lunar observations. Drawing on their pre-mission training in geology and imaging, they described, photographed and marvelled at the Moon, observing parts of its far side that no human had seen in sunlight before.

After a few hours, I was gently hustled out of the science-evaluation room to make space for others. So I didn’t see the scientists’ faces when the astronauts reported seeing green and brown hues on the lunar surface, rather than just greys, or when Glover rhapsodized about the shadows along the ‘terminator’, the boundary between day and night on the Moon.

Marie Henderson, another leader of the Artemis II science team, smiles as the astronauts express their awe of the Moon.Credit: NASA/Luna Posadas Nava

A later news conference revealed that there were “audible screams of delight” in the room when Wiseman reported that the astronauts had seen multiple ‘impact flashes’, created when tiny meteorites pelted the lunar surface.

More observations flooded in during the remainder of the fly-by. The Artemis II crew saw mountains and cliffs. They saw features shaped like human hand prints and dinosaur footprints. Towards the end of the passage, they witnessed a total solar eclipse for nearly an hour. “There’s absolutely no words to describe what we are looking at out this window,” Wiseman said.

The rush of imagery

As the fly-by drew to a close, we journalists waited not-so-patiently to see the images the astronauts had recorded. We would have to hang on until morning. They were transmitted overnight Houston time, and NASA’s image-processing wizards worked their magic in the wee hours of Tuesday to get them ready for public viewing. I talked to David Hollibaugh Baker, a NASA planetary geologist who helps to lead the archiving of the mission’s data. He had been on a sleep shift for the first part of the night, and arrived at the space centre after the first images were up on the computer displays. “I opened up the door, and there was the Moon,” he said. “My heart was racing … I never felt that kind of rush before.”

The image that hit him on entering the room showed the Moon eclipsing the Sun, with an ethereal glow radiating into space from around the darkened lunar disk. Planets from Mercury to Neptune studded the surrounding blackness of space like a line of diamond chips.

Artemis II scientists work in the mission’s scientific nerve centre, a room where live data and observations flow from the astronauts back to Earth.Credit: NASA/Bill Stafford

And that was it. By Tuesday afternoon, with the fly-by over and the data now in hand, the scientists were ready to get to work.

I sat in the room once more, this time to observe a meeting between the astronauts and the scientists. The researchers had a precious 20 minutes to ask the Artemis II crew follow-up questions about their lunar observations, before the astronauts’ memories faded. “This is going to be tightly choreographed,” said Jacob Richardson, NASA’s deputy lunar-science lead who was now in charge.

Capturing memories

The questions flowed into space, relayed by the mission lunar-science lead, NASA geologist Kelsey Young. Had the astronauts really seen impact flashes, or could the bursts of light in their vision have been caused by cosmic rays? No, the astronauts said. They knew what cosmic-ray flashes looked like. “I definitely see a dozen or two every night when I’m going to sleep,” one of them said. “The impact flashes were absolutely different.”
articles.reasoning: The article describes the successful and historic Artemis II mission's lunar fly-by, highlighting human exploration achievements, scientific observations of the Moon's far side, and the excitement and collaboration of the science team. This represents a significant breakthrough in space exploration with broad public interest and inspiring impact.
articles.personality_title: Artemis II astronauts share stunning Moon views during historic fly-by
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA's Artemis II mission sent four astronauts around the far side of the Moon, marking an important step in human space exploration. Scientists on Earth were closely involved, watching and studying the observations made during this trip.

**What happened** – On April 6, 2026, astronauts Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen flew aboard the spacecraft named Integrity. During a nearly seven-hour lunar fly-by, they observed and photographed parts of the Moon’s far side that no humans had ever seen in sunlight before. Their live reports included descriptions of colorful areas on the Moon, shadow details, and even flashes caused by tiny meteorites hitting the surface.

**Impact** – This mission gave scientists new and rare views of the Moon’s surface, including colors and shapes never recorded before. Seeing impact flashes confirmed ongoing meteorite activity on the Moon. The astronauts’ detailed observations, combined with their geology training, helped researchers understand the Moon better. The excitement and teamwork at NASA’s Johnson Space Center showed how human space exploration and science work closely together to learn more about our closest neighbor in space.

**What's next step** – Now that the Artemis II team has returned with fresh data and images, scientists will study the new information to learn more about the Moon’s surface and history. This mission sets the stage for future Artemis missions, which aim to send astronauts to the Moon’s surface again and eventually prepare for human missions to Mars.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Artemis II’s successful Moon fly-by brought back new, exciting views and discoveries that deepen our understanding of the Moon and guide future space exploration.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les astronautes d’Artemis II partagent des vues étonnantes de la Lune lors du survol historique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La mission Artemis II de la NASA a envoyé quatre astronautes autour de la face cachée de la Lune, une étape importante dans l’exploration spatiale humaine. Sur Terre, des scientifiques suivaient de près les observations faites pendant ce voyage.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 6 avril 2026, les astronautes Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch et Jeremy Hansen ont volé à bord du vaisseau nommé Integrity. Pendant un survol lunaire de près de sept heures, ils ont observé et photographié des parties de la face cachée de la Lune jamais vues auparavant en plein jour par des humains. Ils ont rapporté en direct des descriptions de zones colorées, des détails d’ombres et même des éclairs causés par de petits météorites frappant la surface.

**Impact** – Cette mission a offert aux scientifiques des vues nouvelles et rares de la surface lunaire, avec des couleurs et formes jamais enregistrées. Voir les éclairs d’impact a confirmé une activité météoritique en cours sur la Lune. Les observations détaillées des astronautes, renforcées par leur formation en géologie, ont aidé les chercheurs à mieux comprendre la Lune. L’enthousiasme et la collaboration au centre spatial Johnson de la NASA montrent comment l’exploration spatiale humaine et la science travaillent ensemble pour en apprendre plus sur notre voisin spatial.

**Prochaine étape** – Maintenant que l’équipe d’Artemis II est revenue avec de nouvelles données et images, les scientifiques vont étudier ces informations pour mieux connaître la surface et l’histoire de la Lune. Cette mission prépare aussi le terrain pour les futures missions Artemis, qui visent à renvoyer des astronautes sur la surface lunaire, puis à préparer des missions humaines vers Mars.

**En résumé** – Le survol réussi de la Lune par Artemis II a apporté des vues et découvertes nouvelles et passionnantes qui approfondissent notre compréhension de la Lune et orientent l’exploration spatiale future.
articles.personality_title_es: Los astronautas de Artemis II comparten vistas impresionantes de la Luna durante el histórico sobrevuelo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La misión Artemis II de la NASA envió a cuatro astronautas alrededor del lado oculto de la Luna, un paso importante en la exploración espacial humana. Los científicos en la Tierra seguían de cerca las observaciones hechas durante este viaje.

**Qué pasó** – El 6 de abril de 2026, los astronautas Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch y Jeremy Hansen volaron a bordo de la nave llamada Integrity. Durante un sobrevuelo lunar de casi siete horas, observaron y fotografiaron partes del lado oculto de la Luna que ningún humano había visto antes a la luz del día. Informaron en vivo sobre áreas coloridas, detalles de sombras e incluso destellos causados por pequeños meteoritos que impactaban la superficie.

**Impacto** – Esta misión ofreció a los científicos vistas nuevas y raras de la superficie lunar, con colores y formas nunca antes registradas. Ver los destellos de impacto confirmó la actividad continua de meteoritos en la Luna. Las observaciones detalladas de los astronautas, apoyadas por su entrenamiento en geología, ayudaron a los investigadores a entender mejor la Luna. La emoción y el trabajo en equipo en el Centro Espacial Johnson de la NASA muestran cómo la exploración humana y la ciencia trabajan juntas para aprender más sobre nuestro vecino en el espacio.

**Próximo paso** – Ahora que el equipo de Artemis II ha regresado con nuevos datos e imágenes, los científicos estudiarán esta información para conocer mejor la superficie y la historia de la Luna. Esta misión prepara el camino para futuras misiones Artemis, que buscan enviar astronautas nuevamente a la superficie lunar y eventualmente preparar misiones humanas a Marte.

**Mensaje en una frase** – El exitoso sobrevuelo lunar de Artemis II trajo nuevas y emocionantes vistas y descubrimientos que profundizan nuestro conocimiento de la Luna y guían la exploración espacial futura.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing lunar surface viewed through a spacecraft window, showing subtle green and brown hues, softly illuminated craters and mountains, with four abstract silhouettes representing astronauts gazing in awe; outside, a radiant total solar eclipse glows gently around the Moon’s darkened disk, set against a deep space backdrop dotted with tiny, shining planets like scattered gems.
